title: Four-shaft Bronson lace
subtitle: Weaving a spot-and-net lace structure on a four-shaft loom
excerpt: Bronson lace is a traditional American lace weave producing a net-like ground with solid blocks. This entry covers the threading, tie-up, and treadling for a two-block Bronson lace sample on a four-shaft loom, with guidance on choosing the right sett for an open, readable lace effect.
sourceNotes: Bronson lace documented in Mary Atwater, The Shuttle-Craft Book of American Hand-Weaving (1928, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in Harriet Tidball, Handweaving Patterns from Finland (1966, in-copyright, substance only referenced). The structure dates from 18th-century American handweaving.

glossaryTerms.definition: The pattern by which each warp thread is assigned to a specific shaft. In Bronson lace, shaft 1 is a constant ground shaft; shafts 2 to 4 carry the pattern units.
The number of warp threads per unit of width (expressed as ends per 10 cm or ends per inch). Lace weaves use a more open sett than plain weave to allow the float structure to read as open mesh.
A warp or weft thread that passes over two or more threads of the opposing element without interlacing. In Bronson lace, controlled floats create the net-like openings.

content.text: Bronson lace is a spot-and-net structure in which some groups of warp threads interweave in a lace-like net pattern while others form solid blocks. The key to a readable Bronson lace sample is a more open
than would be used for plain weave at the same grist: the
need space to open into visible holes rather than simply crossing without showing. The
uses shaft 1 as a constant ground thread that appears in every repeat.

items.name: Fine cotton or linen warp yarn (10/2 or 20/2)
Same or finer yarn for weft

content.text: The Bronson lace threading unit for two-block design: 1-2-1-2-1-3-1-3 (block A, using shafts 1 and 2) alternating with 1-4-1-4-1-3-1-3 (block B, using shafts 1 and 4). Shaft 1 appears in every unit as the ground shaft. Thread at a sett about 30% more open than plain weave sett for the chosen yarn.

Tie: treadle 1 to shafts 1 and 3 (net row), treadle 2 to shaft 2 (block A pattern row), treadle 3 to shaft 4 (block B pattern row), treadle 4 to shafts 2 and 4 (tabby 2). Weave: for each block pair, alternate the net-row treadle with the pattern-block treadle, interspersing tabby rows at the block transitions. The treadling sequence is: 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, tabby, 1, 3, 1, 3, 1, 3, tabby (for a two-block alternating design).

Remove the sample from the loom and hand-wash gently. The lace openings will close slightly in washing but reopen when the cloth is stretched slightly while damp and dried flat. Press lightly from the wrong side while still damp.

items.problem: The lace openings are barely visible in the finished cloth
items.cause: The sett is too close for the yarn grist
items.fix: Resample at a more open sett. Bronson lace needs 30 to 40% fewer ends per inch than a balanced plain weave at the same grist to show readable lace holes.

items.problem: The cloth has uneven texture across the width
items.cause: Inconsistent beat pressure across the width of the loom
items.fix: Check that the beater swings freely and evenly. Beat each pick from the wrist, not the elbow, to keep the force consistent across the full warp width.
